coredata.dc:description: Diabetic critical limb ischemia (CLI), a common complication of diabetes with high morbidity and amputation rates, has seriously endangered the patients’ health and quality of life. Diabetic CLI is commonly characterized by inflammatory imbalance, vasculopathy, and neuropathy, which significantly impede tissue regeneration. Most biomaterials offer limited angiogenic functions, of which neuropathy treatment is still in its infancy. Herein, we report a composite conductive hydrogel (MSC@COP) by combining the salidroside-derived carbon dots (CDs)-embedded magnesium silicate nanoflowers (MSC) with the PEDOT:PSS-based hydrogel. Responding to the low-pH environment of diabetic CLI, CDs and magnesium ions (Mg2+) are released to scavenge reactive oxygen species in the ischemic microenvironment and promote the M1-to-M2 polarization of macrophages to reshape the inflammatory environment. Meanwhile, the high conductivity of the hydrogel conferred by PEDOT:PSS not only repairs the endogenous electric field but also synergizes with CDs and Mg2+ to inhibit Schwann cell pyroptosis, improve the recruitment of Schwann cells, consequently promoting the dual reconstruction of neural and vascular tissues.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of MSC and MSC@COP. A) TEM image of MSC. B) Elemental (C, Mg, and Si) mapping images corresponding to high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy images of MSC. C) XRD patterns of MS and MSC. D) XPS full spectrum of MSC. The C 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of E) MSC and F) salidroside. G) SEM image of MSC@COP hydrogel. H) Strain sweep rheology of different hydrogel at 25 ℃. I) The alternate step strain sweep of MSC@COP hydrogel at alternating strain cycles of 1 % and 1000 %.

In vitro evaluation of MSC and MSC@COP hydrogel. A) The reactive oxygen scavenging spectra of •O2 − detected by EPR. B) The SOD-mimic activity of MSC. C) Measurement of O2 released from H2O2 (20 mM) catalyzed by MSC and the inset shows the photo of MSC catalyzing the formation of O2 bubbles after incubation for 5 min. D) The CAT-mimic activity of MSC. E) The reactive oxygen scavenging spectra of •OH were detected by EPR. F) DPPH and G) ABTS+ scavenging rates and photos of the hydrogel with different concentrations. H) The degradation rate of MSC in PBS solution with different pH (pH = 6.0 and 7.4). I) The release curves of Mg2+ and CDs from 5 mg mL−1 MSC@COP hydrogel at different pH (pH = 6.0 and 7.4). J) EIS and K) CV curves of CO, COP, and MSC@COP hydrogel. L) Conductivities of different hydrogels. Data are presented as the mean ± SD. (n = 3). ns = not significant, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

Macrophage behavior regulation function of MSC@COP hydrogel. A) Representative fluorescence images of total ROS (DCFH-DA) and B) the average fluorescence intensity in RAW264.7 cells under different treatments. C) Representative results of flow cytometry analysis showed the expression of iNOS and CD206 in RAW264.7 cells under different treatments and D) quantitative results. E) Representative immunofluorescence images and F) relative fluorescence quantitative results of iNOS and CD206 expression in RAW264.7 cells after different treatments. G) The mRNA expression levels of inflammatory factors (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-10, and ARG-1) in RAW264.7 cells were detected by RT-qPCR. Data are presented as the mean ± SD. (n = 3). ns = not significant, ***p < 0.001.

MSC@COP neurovascular dual repair activity. A) Representative optical microscope images of transwell assay of SCs with different hydrogel treatments. B) Fluorescent images of EDU staining of SCs incubated in hydrogel for 1 day. C) Z-axis superposition projection of SCs in different hydrogels. D) Representative fluorescence images of β-Tubulin Ⅲ staining of DRG neurons cultured with different sample treatments in the co-culture model. E) Quantitative analysis of neurite length. F) Representative optical microscope plots of HUVECs tube formation experiments for different sample treatments in the co-culture model and quantitative analyses of G) branch points and H) total tube length. I) Gene expression of BDNF and VEGF in co-culture systems. J) Graphical representation of MSC@COP promoting neurovascular regeneration by regulating cytokine secretion from SCs. Data are presented as the mean ± SD. (n = 3). ns = not significant, *p < 0.05, ***p < 0.001.

The cytoprotective mechanism of MSC@COP. A) PCA analysis of three groups of SCs sequencing results. Volcano diagrams of B) the Sham and H2O2 groups, C) the H2O2 and MSC@COP groups. D) Pyroptosis-related heatmap among the three groups. E) KEGG pathway enrichment analysis of the H2O2 and MSC@COP groups. F) GSE analysis of NLR pathway between the Sham and H2O2 groups. G) GSE analysis of NLR pathway between the H2O2 and MSC@COP groups. H) Representative fluorescence images of GSDMD-N staining of SCs treated with different treatments. I) The protein levels and J) quantitative results of pyroptosis-related proteins (NLRP3, cleaved caspase-1, GSDMD-N and IL-1β) in SC were detected by western blotting. Data are presented as the mean ± SD. (n = 3). ns = not significant, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

The protective effect of MSC@COP hydrogel on diabetic CLI in vivo. A) Timeline of diabetic CLI mouse modeling and treatment. B) Representative images of ischemic limbs of mice detected by LSPI (red area represents blood flow, blue area represents no blood flow) and C) quantitative data. D) Semi-quantitative clinical evaluation of ischemic injury in ischemic hindlimbs. n = 10. E) The percentage distribution of limb salvage, foot necrosis, and limb loss in each group. n = 10. Data are presented as the mean ± SD. ns = not significant, *p < 0.05, ***p < 0.001.

Inflammatory regulation of MSC@COP hydrogel in vivo. A) Fluorescence staining results of iNOS and CD206 in tissues of each group on days 7 and 21. B) Quantitative results of iNOS-positive cells. C) Quantitative results of CD206-positive cells. D) Representative IHC images and E) quantification of inflammatory factors in tissues on 21st day in each group. F) Expression of TNF-α, IL-6, IL-10, and ARG-1 in tissues. Data are presented as the mean ± SD. (n = 5). ns = not significant, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

MSC@COP in vivo neurovascular reconstruction function. A) Mouse tissue detection diagram. B) H&E staining and C) Masson staining of gastrocnemius tissue sections. D) Representative fluorescence staining results of S100, CD31, and β-Tubulin Ⅲ in tissues on days 7 and 21. E) The fluorescence quantitative results of S100, CD31, and β-Tubulin Ⅲ in tissues. F) Detection of protein expression levels and G) quantification of BDNF and VEGF in tissues by western blotting. H) The protein levels of pyroptosis-related proteins (NLRP3, cleaved caspase-1, GSDMD-N and IL-1β) in tissues were detected by western blotting. Data are presented as the mean ± SD. (n = 3). ns = not significant, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.

Preparation and treatment mechanism of MSC@COP. A) Synthesis process of MSC@COP. B) MSC@COP hydrogel percutaneous deep tissue injection. C) Responsive to low-pH microenvironment, MSC@COP released Mg2+ and CDs, presenting multi-functions: Ⅰ) Scavenging local ROS through superoxide dismutase (SOD)-mimic and catalase (CAT)-mimic activities; Ⅱ) Improving the local inflammatory microenvironment by regulating macrophage polarization, reducing the expression of pro-inflammatory factors, and up-regulating anti-inflammatory factor expression; Ⅲ) Inhibiting the activation of NLRP3-related pyroptosis pathway in SCs and recruiting them to the injured area; Ⅳ) Stimulating SCs to secrete brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) to achieve dual neurovascular reconstruction.

head.ce:title: Conductive hydrogel with responsive release of Herbal-Derived carbon dots for neurovascular reconstruction in diabetic critical limb ischemia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Diabetic critical limb ischemia (CLI), a common complication of diabetes with high morbidity and amputation rates, has seriously endangered the patients’ health and quality of life. Diabetic CLI is commonly characterized by inflammatory imbalance, vasculopathy, and neuropathy, which significantly impede tissue regeneration. Most biomaterials offer limited angiogenic functions, of which neuropathy treatment is still in its infancy. Herein, we report a composite conductive hydrogel (MSC@COP) by combining the salidroside-derived carbon dots (CDs)-embedded magnesium silicate nanoflowers (MSC) with the PEDOT:PSS-based hydrogel. Responding to the low-pH environment of diabetic CLI, CDs and magnesium ions (Mg2+) are released to scavenge reactive oxygen species in the ischemic microenvironment and promote the M1-to-M2 polarization of macrophages to reshape the inflammatory environment. Meanwhile, the high conductivity of the hydrogel conferred by PEDOT:PSS not only repairs the endogenous electric field but also synergizes with CDs and Mg2+ to inhibit Schwann cell pyroptosis, improve the recruitment of Schwann cells, consequently promoting the dual reconstruction of neural and vascular tissues.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetic critical limb ischemia (CLI), a common complication of diabetes that affects over 100 million people globally [1–3], presents a significant challenge in terms of tissue regeneration and has a poor prognosis due to several pathological changes, including oxidative stress, inflammatory imbalance, diabetic vasculopathy, and neuropathy [4–7]. However, there is no effective clinical treatment for diabetic CLI. Currently, biomaterials that exhibit a microenvironment-responsive pattern demonstrate remarkable potential for promoting tissue protection and angiogenesis [8], offering a promising strategy for addressing diabetic CLI [9–11]. It is worth noting that neuropathy and vasculopathy frequently coexist in diabetic CLI, and interact through various mechanisms to hasten disease progression and deterioration [12]. Therefore, biomaterials that focus solely on angiogenesis therapy may not be sufficient for addressing diabetic CLI. In contrast, the neuropathy treatment in diabetic CLI is still in its infancy. Several Schwann cell (SC)-based functional hydrogels have been reported in the field of bone defects and peripheral nerve injury by dual neurovascular reconstruction [13–15]. However, the high-glycemic, ischemic, and inflammatory environment of diabetic CLI induce SCs oxidation and inflammatory stress, and pyroptosis, limiting SCs to promote neurovascular repair [16,17]. Hence, it is urgent to develop a multifunctional hydrogel treatment platform with three capabilities: 1) responding to diabetic CLI microenvironment, 2) regulating inflammatory microenvironment, and 3) promoting the dual neurovascular reconstruction.

During the acute phase of diabetic CLI, ischemia with a high-glycemic environment can cause a localized increase in lactate and then a significant decrease in pH [18]. Consequently, targeting the low-pH environment of diabetic CLI may be a promising strategy for responsive delivery. Magnesium silicate nanoflowers (MS), with a large specific surface area and low-pH induced decomposition, can act as a promising candidates for drug delivery systems specifically designed to respond to the acidic environment in diabetic CLI [19]. Oxidative stress and inflammatory imbalance in diabetic CLI mainly contribute to cellular dysfunction and challenges in tissue regeneration [20]. Salidroside, an herbal monomer extracted from traditional Chinese medicine Rhodiola, possesses antioxidant and anti-inflammatory functions and inhibits pyroptosis-related pathways in cells [21–26]. Accordingly, salidroside is expected to prevent SC pyroptosis in diabetic CLI, restore the reparative function of SCs and then achieve neurovascular reconstruction. To further improve the reconstructive effect, conductive biomaterials can be combined [27,28]. This can rebuild the endogenous bioelectric field in the injured tissue while recruiting SCs to release vasoactive and neurotrophic factors [29,30], further promoting tissue neurovascular regeneration.

Herein, we proposed a conductive composite hydrogel system (MSC@COP) for the treatment of diabetic CLI. In the synthesis of MSC@COP, the salidroside-derived carbon dots (CDs) were first loaded on the surface of magnesium silicate carriers to obtain MgSiO3@CDs (MSC), and further combined with carboxymethyl chitosan/oxidized chondroitin sulfate/PEDOT:PSS (COP) hydrogel (Scheme 1
A). MSC@COP had the following advantages: 1) Providing a favorable regenerative environment for damaged cells: The amino groups of carboxymethyl chitosan and the aldehyde groups of chondroitin sulfate oxide formed a dynamic covalent network of Schiff base bonds to provide injectability and extracellular matrix similarity [31]. Besides, with chemical stability, dielectric properties, and biocompatibility [32,33], PEDOT:PSS provided the hydrogel with excellent conductivity to repair damaged cells (Scheme 1B). 2) Controlled release of CDs and magnesium ions (Mg2+): Responding to the low-pH environment of diabetic CLI, MSC@COP rapidly decomposed and released CDs and Mg2+. 3) Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties: The formation of CDs increased the bioavailability of salidroside and endowed antioxidant nano-enzyme activities, which improved local reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging (Scheme 1C-Ⅰ) [34–36]. Meanwhile, CDs promoted the polarization of M1 macrophages to M2 macrophages, reduced pro-inflammatory factors, and increased anti-inflammatory factors, which reshaped the inflammatory microenvironment (Scheme 1C-Ⅱ). 4) Anti-pyroptosis and neurovascular reconstruction capability: CDs and Mg2+ combined with the excellent conductivity of the hydrogel protected SCs by inhibiting the activation of NLRP3-related pyroptosis pathway (Scheme 1C-Ⅲ). SCs were further recruited to release brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), ultimately achieving neurovascular reconstruction (Scheme 1C-Ⅳ). In conclusion, the conductive MSC@COP hydrogel system we proposed possessed multiple properties of antioxidant, anti-inflammation, and neurovascular reconstruction, which might be an attractive alternative strategy for diabetic CLI.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98.0 %), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), salidroside (98 %), tetraethyl silicate (> 99 %), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl, 99.5 %), ammonia (NH3·H2O, 28 wt%), and chondroitin sulfate (CS, 95 %) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Carboxymethyl chitosan (BR) and PEDOT:PSS (1.5 wt%) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 2′,7′‐dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), Calcein-acetoxymethyl ester/Propidium iodide (Calcein-AM/PI) double staining kit, mitochondrial membrane potential assay kit with JC-1, and cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) were obtained from Solarbio Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) and Dulbecco’s modified eagle culture medium (DMEM) were purchased from HyClone (Beijing, China). Besides, we also used RIPA buffer (BL504A, Biosharp), BCA protein analysis kit (Thermo Fisher, USA), anti-NLRP3, anti-cleaved caspase-1, anti-GSDMD-N, anti-IL-1β, and anti-β-actin (GB11001, Serivicebio), 4 % paraformaldehyde solution (PFA, Thermo Scientific).

To prepare MS, we first synthesized silicon dioxide (SiO2) particles based on previous experience [37]. In short, 150 mL of anhydrous ethanol, 4 mL of deionized water and 12 mL of ammonia were uniformly mixed, and then slowly added into the mixture of 5 mL of tetraethyl orthosilicate and 50 mL of ethanol, and then centrifuged, washed, and lyophilized after 8 h of reaction at room temperature to produce SiO2 pellets. 100 mg SiO2, 339 mg MgCl2, 535 mg NH4Cl and 1 mL NH3·H2O were dissolved in 60 mL water, and the precipitate was prepared by hydrothermal reaction (140 °C, 24 h) in a hydrothermal kettle. Then the precipitate was washed with deionized water to obtain porous hollow MS.

1 g of salidroside and 100 mg of MS were dispersed in 60 mL of ultrapure water and placed in a hydrothermal kettle. After the reaction at 180 °C for 24 h, MSC was prepared by washing and lyophilisation.

To prepare MSC@COP, we first synthesized oxidized chondroitin sulfate (OCS) according to a previously reported [38]. Briefly, 5 g CS was completely dissolved in 100 mL deionized water, and 3 g sodium periodate was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water and added to the CS solution in the dark. After 6 h of reaction, 4 mL ethylene glycol was added to the reaction system to terminate the reaction. Then the above solution was placed in a dialysis bag (3500 MW) and dialyzed for 24 h with water changed every 6 h, and finally freeze-dried to obtain OCS powder.

Further, 400 mg of OCS was dissolved in 3 mL of deionized water. Then 1 mL of a 1.5 % PEDOT:PSS solution was added and stirred evenly. After that, different masses of MSC and 1 mL of a 30 mg mL−1 carboxymethyl chitosan aqueous solution were quickly mixed with OCS solution to form the hydrogel with a final PEDOT:PSS concentration of 0.3 %.

The morphology of the materials was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, ZEISS, Sigma 300, Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Thermo Fisher, Inspect F50, USA). The elements of the synthesized materials were determined by high angle annular dark field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM, FEI, Titan G2 60–300, USA) and X-ray energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). Zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, Zetasizer Ultra, UK) was used to monitor the potential of the material, and the scavenging ability of the material to •O2 – and •OH at different times was evaluated by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, Bruker, ELEXSYS E580, USA).

The crystal structure of the samples were confirmed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker, D8ADVANCE, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher, ESCALAB250 Xi, USA), respectively. Fourier transform infrared spectra were collected on a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo Nicolet, Nicolet 5700, USA) to analyze the chemical structure of the resulting hydrogel. Rheological measurements were performed by a rheometer (Anton Paar, MCR92, Germany), and the “NCU” shape was formed by needle injection of MSC@COP hydrogel. The electrochemical properties of the hydrogels were measured by an electrochemical workstation (Metrohm, Autolab PGSTAT302N, Switzerland).

The cells were seeded into 96-well plates at a density of 2.0 × 103 per well, and then cultured in an incubator (37 °C, 5 % CO2) for 24 h. After the cells adhered to the plate, the medium in each well containing the cells was replaced with 100 μL of solution containing different materials and incubated for days 1, 2, and 3. Subsequently, the original medium was replaced with 100 μL CCK-8 (10 %, v v−1) for 0.5 h, and the absorbance value of each hole at 450 nm was measured by a multifunctional microplate reader. SCs viability was calculated by the following formula:

Cell viability (%) =
A
A 0
× 100 %

(
A 0
is the absorbance of the Control group, A is the absorbance of the experimental group).

In addition, the cytocompatibility was further evaluated by live and dead cell staining. Briefly, SCs were seeded in 24-well culture plates at the density of 2 × 104 cells per well and then co-cultured with 1 mL of impregnation solution containing the samples at different concentrations for another 24 h. Afterward, the cells were stained with Calcein-acetoxymethyl ester/Propidium iodide (Calcein-AM/PI) for 15 min in the dark at 37 °C and visualized by the inverted fluorescence microscope (IX 83, Olympus, Japan).

Endogenous oxidative stress in mouse mononuclear macrophages RAW264.7 cells were induced by LPS from bacteria. RAW264.7 cells were inoculated in 24-well plates at a density of 5 × 105 per well and incubated for 24 h. Then, 100 μL DMEM containing 10μg mL−1 LPS and other different types of samples were added and co-cultured for 4 h. Afterward, the original medium was replaced with 2,7-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) and then co-cultured with cells for 30 min. The cells were observed by an inverted fluorescence microscope. Similarly, HUVECs were cultured at 1 × 106 per well for 24 h, and then co-cultured with DMEM medium containing 0.5 μmol mL−1 H2O2 for 8 h for DCFH-DA fluorescence detection.

RAW264.7 cells were seeded on six-well plates with cell slides at a density of 1 × 106 cells per well and incubated for 24 h. After 8 h of treatment with 10 μg mL−1 LPS, cells were pretreated with 200 μg mL−1 different samples for 24 h. The cells were then fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde solution for 15 min, washed with PBS multiple times, and blocked with 3 % bovine serum albumin (BSA, Servicebio) for 30 min. iNOS (1:100 dilution, Servicebio) and CD206 main antibody (1:200 dilution, Servicebio) were co-cultured with cells at 4 °C overnight and then washed with PBS. Next, the sample was co-cultured with Cy3-labeled goat anti-rabbit IgG antibody (1:300 dilution, Servicebio) for 1 h. After washing three times, the nuclei were stained with 4′,6-diamino-2-phenylindidine (DAPI). Finally, they were observed by flow cytometry (Sysmex, CyFlow Cube 8) and fluorescence microscopy (IX 83, Olympus, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of inflammation-related cytokines in vitro
ce:section.ce:para:
The mRNA expression levels of inflammatory-related cytokines TNF-α, IL-6, IL-10, and ARG-1 were determined by RT-qPCR. RAW264.7 cells were treated with 10 μg mL−1 LPS for 8 h and then pretreated with 200 μg mL−1 different samples for 48 h. Then RAW264.7 cells were collected for further use. First, RNA was extracted according to the instructions of the RNA isolation kit. Reverse transcription was performed with 1 μg RNA. Then cDNA was extracted for qPCR detection. The primer sequences for RT-qPCR analysis are shown in the table.
Primer names Primer sequence (5′-3′)
TNF-α CCCTCACACTCACAAACCACC
CTTTGAGATCCATGCCGTTG
IL-6 GGTGTTGCCTGCTGCCTTCC
GCTCTGGCTTGTTCCTCACTACTC
IL-10 CACTGCTATGCTGCCTGCTCT
GTCGGTTAGCAGTATGTTGTCCAG
ARG-1 CTGGGGATTGGCAAGGTGAT
CAGCCCGTCGACATCAAAG

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3D cell culture of SCs in hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: In short, SCs were mixed with 500 μL of different hydrogels (CO, COP, and MSC@COP) at a density of 1 × 106 mL−1, and incubated at 37 °C, 5 % CO2 for 24 h. Finally, the cells were stained with Calcein-AM/PI in the dark for 15 min and observed using a laser confocal microscope (LSM800, Carl Zeiss) to detect the activity of SCs on 1st day.

Different hydrogels were placed in a 24-well plate, and then the 24-well transwell chamber was placed in the plate, and SCs were inoculated in the upper chamber. After 24 h of culture, the cells were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde, and stained with crystal violet. Then the cells that had not migrated to the lower membrane were removed with a cotton swab, and the macrophages that had migrated to the lower membrane were observed and recorded under a microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scratch test of HUVECs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: In the scratch test of HUVECs, HUVECs were inoculated in the lower chamber of transwell at a density of 1 × 105 per well for 1 day. After the cells adhered to the wall, they were scraped in a straight line with a 200 μL pipette tip and co-cultured with transwell in different groups. The migration of the cells was observed, and the migration of the cells was recorded using a microscope at 0 and 12 h.

SCs were incubated with different sterilized materials (Sham, H2O2, and H2O2 + MSC@COP) for 8 h. Subsequently, the cell precipitates were collected by centrifugation (3000 r min−1, 8 min) and washed thrice with PBS. Finally, the above cell samples were sent to Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China) for gene expression analysis.

The logarithmic phase SCs were inoculated on a 24-well cell culture plate at a density of 1 × 105 cells mL−1 overnight and incubated with 0.5 μmol mL−1 H2O2 for 8 h. Then the medium was replaced, and different materials were incubated for 24 h. The medium was removed, and the cells were rinsed with PBS for 3 times. The treated cells were collected, and RIPA buffer (BL504A, Biosharp) containing protease and phosphatase inhibitors was added to extract total protein from tissues and cells. Then the protein concentration was determined by BCA protein analysis kit (Thermo Fisher, USA). Sodium dodecyl sulfate–polyacrylamide hydrogel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) was used to separate 20 μg protein from each group and transferred to polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, IPVH00010, Millipore) membranes. Subsequently, the membrane was blocked with 5 % bovine serum albumin (BSA) for 1 h at room temperature. Then they were incubated with anti-NLRP3, anti-cleaved casepase-1, anti-GSDMD-N, anti-IL-1β, and anti-β-actin (GB11001, Serivicebio) at 4 °C overnight. Then the membrane was incubated with the secondary antibody at 37 °C for 60 min. The protein bands were visualized using an integrated chemiluminescence instrument ChemiDocXRS (BioRad), and the band intensity was quantified using Image J software.
The experimental procedures followed the institutional guidelines for animal care, and were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Nanchang University (Nanchang, China; NCULAE-20221228034).

Firstly, eight-week-old male mice purchased from Hangzhou Ziyuan Experimental Animal Technology were selected and intraperitoneal injected of streptozotocin (50 mg kg−1, 5 days) to establish the diabetic model. Severe limb ischemia surgery was performed on the previously modeled diabetic mice. In short, one hind limb of each anesthetized mouse was ligated with 6.0 silk sutures at the proximal and distal ends of the left femoral artery and vein, and the blood vessels were completely cut between the two sutures. The contralateral hind limbs were used as controls. 4 h after surgery, the mice were randomly divided into 4 groups (n ≥ 10 in each group). Each group was intramuscular injected with 200 μL PBS (Model group), 200 μL CO hydrogel (CO group), 200 μL COP hydrogel (COP group), and 200 μL MSC@COP hydrogel (MSC@COP group).

The blood perfusion of the hind limbs with and without surgery was monitored using a Laser Doppler Blood Flow instrument (SIM Opto-Technology Co., Ltd., Wuhan, China). On the 0, 7th, 14th, and 21st day. The generated images were analyzed to quantify blood perfusion. Semi-quantitative clinical evaluation of ischemic injury of ischemic hind limbs was performed, and the percentage of limb salvage, foot necrosis and limb loss in each group was quantified.

Limb functional scores were used to quantify the ischemic damage (0 represents no difference with contralateral hindlimb; 1 represents mild discoloration; 2 represents moderate discoloration; 3 represents severe discoloration or partial tissue loss; 4 represents amputation) and ambulatory damage (0 represents toes flexion; 1 represents plantar flexion; 2 represents no plantar flexion but also no dragging of foot; 3 represents dragging of foot) [39].

The mice were sacrificed, and the ischemic hind limbs were collected. The harvested hind limbs were fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde solution (PFA, Thermo Scientific), embedded in paraffin, and cut into 5 μm sections for histological and immunohistochemical analysis. After conventional dewaxing to water, paraffin sections were subjected to H&E and Masson trichrome staining. Subsequently, histological sections were observed and imaged with an ordinary optical microscope.

For immunohistochemical staining, paraffin sections were dewaxed and subjected to heat-induced epitope repair in ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) buffer (pH = 8), blocked with 10 % goat serum and 0.3 % Triton X-100 in 1 × DPBS, and then incubated with primary antibody at 4 °C overnight. Antibodies included anti-iNOS antibody, anti-CD206 antibody, anti-S100 antibody, anti-CD31 antibody, anti-β-Tubulin antibody, anti-TNF-α antibody, and anti-IL-10 antibody. After the corresponding secondary antibody coupling and DAPI staining, the tissue sections were imaged with a confocal microscope and quantitatively analyzed.

All experiments were repeated at least 3 times, and the data were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) were performed using GraphPad Prism 9.4.1 software. ns represents no statistical difference, *p < 0.05; **p < 0.01; ***p < 0.001. GraphPad Prism 9.4.1 (GraphPad Software, USA) was used for graphical analysis.

MSC was synthesized by a two-step hydrothermal reaction, and the successful fabrication of flower-like MS nanoparticles with a particle size of approximately 220 nm was confirmed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Fig. S1, Supplementary material). CDs were synthesized from salidroside in a hydrothermal reaction, and the average diameter of CDs was about 3.8 ± 0.6 nm by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. S2, Supplementary material). TEM and SEM images of MSC confirm the successful coating of CDs and the integrity of MS structure (Fig. 1
A and S3, Supplementary material). After the CDs modification, the average size of particles increased from 255.7 ± 85.6 nm for MS to 296.3 ± 119.8 nm for MSC, and the zeta potential of MSC raised to − 21.9 ± 3.5 mV (Fig. S4 and S5, Supplementary material). As for the elemental compositions of MSC, the carbon (C) element from CDs as well as the silicon (Si) and magnesium (Mg) elements from MS were detected, further indicating the successful coating of CDs (Fig. 1B). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) spectrum of MSC exhibited characteristic peaks of MS as well as CDs, in which the intensity of MS peaks was decreased possibly for CDs-coating (Fig. 1C and S6, Supplementary material). The chemical composition and valence states of the elements in MSC were then analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. 1D). The C 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of MSC and salidroside could be well fitted by three subpeaks at 284.8, 286, and 289 eV according to C=C/C–C, C–O, and C=O, respectively, which proved the increased degree of oxidation increased after polymerization (Fig. 1E and 1F).

MSC@COP hydrogel was prepared by mixing carboxymethyl chitosan (CMC), oxidized chondroitin sulfate (OCS), PEDOT:PSS, and MSC at room temperature. The MSC@COP hydrogel showed a three-dimensional porous structure (Fig. 1G). The macroporous structure facilitated cell migration and proliferation, as well as oxygen and nutrient transport [40].

In the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum of CO hydrogel, the peak at 1631 cm−1 corresponded to the C=N stretching vibration, suggesting the formation of an imine bond (Fig. S7, Supplementary material) [41]. After the introduction of PEDOT/PSS, the peak of C=N stretching vibration was shifted to 1620 cm−1, which was attributed to the C=C stretching vibration in aromatic nucleus, and peaks at the C–S–C bond appear at 976 cm−1, 840 cm−1, and 682 cm−1 [42]. Besides, the appearance of Si–O bending vibration at 454 cm−1 confirmed the preparation of MSC@COP. The hydrogel was composed of a dynamically reversible Schiff base network formed with amino and aldehyde groups. The EDS spectrum confirmed the uniform distribution of various elements on the surface of the hydrogel (Fig. S8, Supplementary material). At the same time, the addition of PEDOT:PSS and MSC did not affect the formation of MSC@COP hydrogel with good injectability (Fig. S9, Supplementary material). According to the rheological analysis (Fig. 1H), the addition of PEDOT:PSS and MSC did not significantly change the storage modulus (G') and loss modulus (G''). The storage modulus (G' = 205.9 Pa) was 12 times higher than the loss modulus (G'' = 16.5 Pa) over the oscillation frequencies ranging from 0.1 % to 1000 %. The G'' exceeded G' for the hydrogel when the oscillatory strain exceeded 400 %, indicating a sol–gel transition. However, the hydrogel structure collapsed and was rapidly recovered by four cycles of 1 % and 1000 % stress (Fig. 1I), which suggested that the hydrogel based on a dynamically reversible covalent Schiff base network had excellent self-healing and shear thinning abilities. The results could also explain the good injectability of hydrogel and demonstrate its potential for application in deep tissues. Meanwhile, the swelling ratios of hydrogels were carried out by immersing the hydrogels in PBS at 37 °C (Fig. S10, Supplementary material). Interestingly, all groups of hydrogel showed similar swelling ratios, suggesting a small effect of PEDOT:PSS and MSC on the swelling properties of the hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro evaluation of MSC and MSC@COP hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para:
Overproduction of ROS in diabetic CLI can cause oxidative damage to cells and tissues and inhibit cell regeneration [43]. Superoxide dismutase (SOD), as one of the intracellular enzymes, can scavenge excessive ROS in tissues. We evaluated the SOD-mimic activity of MSC by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy. The xanthine/xanthine oxidase system was used to produce superoxide anions (•O2 −), which could be trapped by using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin trap. After the addition of 200 μg mL−1 MSC, the •O2 − signal was significantly reduced (Fig. 2
A). Subsequently, the SOD-mimic activity was evaluated using a SOD kit, as depicted in Fig. 2B. The results indicated that the inhibition rate was augmented with the increasing concentration of MSCs after adding the reaction reagent for 20 minutes (min), suggesting that MSCs possess robust SOD-mimic activity. The catalase (CAT)-mimic activity of MSC was evaluated by dissolved oxygen content and CAT activity assay kit. Oxygen (O2) bubbles were rapidly generated after adding MSC to the H2O2 solution, and the O2 content (25.61 mg mL−1) produced by 100 μg mL−1 MSC was nearly 3 times than that without MSC after 10 min of reaction (Fig. 2C). The CAT-mimic catalytic rate of MSC reached 61.12 % at 100 μg mL−1 within 5 min (Fig. 2D). The outcomes demonstrated that MSC had a mimic SOD/CAT cascade antioxidative enzyme activity and the resulting O2 had the potential to alleviate the effects of hypoxia. Notably, •OH is also the main ROS that aggravates the oxidative damage in diabetes [44]. Through the EPR detection of •OH, we found that the spectral signal was significantly reduced after the addition of MSC, indicating that MSC had an excellent ability to scavenge •OH (Fig. 2E).

The effect of MSC concentration on oxygen radicals scavenging was systematically studied by 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) assay and 2,2′‐azino‐bis(3‐ethylbenzothiazoline‐6‐sulfonic acid) (ABTS) assay (Fig. 2F and 2G). After incubation with different concentrations (0.5 mg mL−1, 1 mg mL−1, 2.5 mg mL−1, 5 mg mL−1, and 10 mg mL−1) of MSC@COP hydrogel for 30 min, more than 80 % of DPPH and ABTS radicals were eliminated by 5 mg mL−1 MSC@COP, which exhibited the strong radicals scavenging ability and the optimal concentration for subsequent experiments.

In early stages of diabetic CLI, glucose metabolism disorder, decreased blood perfusion and the excessive accumulation of lactic acid induce a decrease in pH at the ischemic area [18]. Responsive drug release helps to accelerate tissue repair [2]. We further verified the ability of MSC to respond to the low-pH environment in diabetic CLI tissue. SEM images showed that, compared with the neutral (pH = 7.4) environment, the structure disintegrated faster under acidic condition (pH = 6.0) (Fig. S11, Supplementary material). Statistically, after 3 weeks of immersion, the mean degradation rate in the neutral and acidic solutions were 49.54 % and 76.31 %, respectively (Fig. 2H). Meanwhile, the release rates of Mg2+ and CDs from hydrogel were detected at different pH (pH = 6.0 and 7.4). The CDs had an obvious ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption peak at 275 nm, which was employed to establish a concentration standard curve (Fig. S12, Supplementary material). Then, plasma spectrometry and UV–vis absorption spectral analysis of the leaching solution confirmed that the responsive release behavior of Mg2+ and CDs in the hydrogel was consistent with the degradation curve of MSC (Fig. 2I).

The blood compatibility of CO, COP, and MSC@COP hydrogel was studied by hemolysis test (Fig. S13, Supplementary material). Quantitative analysis showed that the hemolysis rate was less than 5 % in all experimental groups, indicating the favorable blood compatibility of hydrogel. In both in vivo and in vitro environments, we explored the degradation of the hydrogel through experiments such as Doppler ultrasound, and the results demonstrated its good degradability (Fig. S14, Supplementary material). Then we used SCs to evaluate the cytocompatibility of different hydrogels by cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. As shown in Fig. S15 (Supplementary material), SCs exhibited good growth and proliferation behavior after incubation with 5 mg mL−1 MSC@COP hydrogel extracts for 1 to 3 days, indicating the excellent cytocompatibility of COP and MSC@COP hydrogel, which was further confirmed by live/dead staining (Fig. S16, Supplementary material).

We further explored the electrical conductivity of the MSC@COP hydrogel. The impedance of different hydrogels was detected by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The Nyquist plot containing the semicircle reflected the charge transfer resistance, with larger semicircle diameters indicating higher resistance [45]. As shown in Fig. 2J, the semicircular diameter of the hydrogel was significantly reduced by the addition of PEDOT:PSS, representing an increase in hydrogel conductivity. The conductivity of different hydrogels was further explored by cyclic voltammetry (CV). Consistent with the Nyquist plot, the oxidation peak of the hydrogel increased in the CV curve after the addition of PEDOT:PSS, thereby indicating an enhancement in the mobility of electrons and ions (Fig. 2K). The four-point probe results highlighted the difference in conductivity between the CO hydrogel (0.14 mS cm−1) and the MSC@COP hydrogel (1.42 mS cm−1) (Fig. 2L). In addition, we connected the hydrogel in series with the LED bulb. The MSC@COP hydrogel could successfully conduct electricity and light up the LED, which could not succeed with the CO hydrogel (Fig. S17, Supplementary material). All of the above confirmed the good conductivity of MSC@COP hydrogel, which was favorable for promoting rapid intercellular communication through endogenous bioelectrical signals generated by ion channels or cell-to-cell contacts [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS scavenging and Macrophage behavior regulation of MSC@COP hydrogel in vitro
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to evaluate the intracellular ROS scavenging ability of MSC@COP hydrogel, we constructed a cellular inflammatory model using lipopolysaccharide (LPS) stimulated RAW264.7 cells based on previous experience, and used 2′,7′‐dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH‐DA) probe to co-incubate with cells to label ROS [46]. The results showed that the intracellular ROS level significantly increased after co-culture of LPS with RAW264.7 for 4 hours (h), whereas the MSC@COP group exhibited a strong ROS scavenging effect (Fig. 3
A, 3B, and S18A, Supplementary material). Similarly, in the inflammatory model of human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) co-cultured with H2O2, the MSC@COP group also exhibited a strong scavenging effect (Fig. S18B, Supplementary material).

Macrophage polarization regulates injured tissue repair [47–50]. However, in ischemic limbs, excessive activation of pro-inflammatory macrophages leads to inflammatory imbalance and inhibits tissue regeneration [51,52]. Therefore, regulating macrophage behavior and improving inflammatory imbalance play an important role in repairing ischemic limb tissue. In order to study the effect of MSC@COP on macrophage polarization, RAW264.7 cells were treated with LPS for 8 h, and further co-cultured with hydrogels for 24 h. The effect of hydrogel on macrophage polarization was investigated by flow cytometry and immunofluorescence staining. The results showed no significant difference in the number of M1 macrophages (CD11b and iNOS positive) and M2 macrophages (CD11b and CD206 positive) between the LPS and the COP group (Fig. 3C and 3D). However, in the MSC@COP group, the number of M1 macrophages decreased and the proportion of M2 macrophages increased. The M1 macrophage marker iNOS (red) and M2 macrophage marker CD206 (green) were observed by immunofluorescence staining under inflammatory conditions. Compared with the LPS group, the MSC@COP group had weaker iNOS and stronger CD206 fluorescence signals, indicating enhanced M2 activation (Fig. 3E, 3F, and S19, Supplementary material).

Interleukin-6 (IL-6), tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α), interleukin-10 (IL-10) and arginase 1 (ARG-1) are inflammatory cytokines closely associated with diabetic CLI [4]. The expression of these inflammatory-related genes were detected by reverse transcription-quantitative polymerase chain reaction (RT-qPCR) after 2 days of co-culture (Fig. 3G). The results showed that the addition of LPS promoted the expression of pro-inflammatory genes (IL-6 and TNF-α). The expression of pro-inflammatory genes in macrophages was inhibited after co-culture with MSC@COP hydrogel, wheraes the expression of anti-inflammatory genes (IL-10 and ARG-1) was significantly enhanced. The above results suggested that MSC@COP hydrogel effectively removed intracellular ROS in an inflammatory environment, corrected the imbalance of inflammation, and provided a favorable microenvironment for tissue regeneration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SC protection and neurovascular Co-Repair activity of MSC@COP hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para:
SC is the main glial cell of peripheral nerves, which is activated after the onset of injury, and then migrates and increases directionally in the injured tissue, mediating nerve growth and vascular regeneration [13]. The ability of hydrogel to recruit SCs and promote proliferation was further demonstrated by transwell transmembrane culture. The results showed that both MSC@COP could recruit SCs, while no significant cell migration was detected in the CO and COP-treated groups, suggesting that MSC@COP might be able to recruit SCs to the site of injury by releasing Mg2+ (Fig. 4
A and S20, Supplementary material). Immunofluorescence staining of 5-ethynyl-2-deoxyuridine (EDU) showed higher proliferation vitality in SCs cultured with the COP hydrogel and the MSC@COP hydrogel than with the CO hydrogel (Fig. 4B and S21, Supplementary material). Meanwhile, to visualize the survival status of the cells in the hydrogel, the SCs were encapsulated in the hydrogel for 3D cell culture. After 24 h of incubation, SCs showed a uniform punctate distribution within the hydrogel (Fig. 4C and S22, Supplementary material), and the cells were in a good growth state. In addition, there were more cells in the COP hydrogel than in the CO hydrogel, because the increase in the conductivity after the addition of PEDOT:PSS was more favorable for cell adhesion to the hydrogel. Besides, the cell density was higher in the MSC@COP group than in the COP group, suggesting that the release of Mg2+ synergized with the increase in high conductivity to promote cell proliferation.

Recent studies found that bioactive engineered hydrogel can promote neural and vascular regeneration in bone-deficient environments through activation of SCs [14,53], but hyperglycemia-driven SC stress and dysfunction in diabetic CLI limit its application in diabetes mellitus [17]. To further study the protective effect of MSC@COP hydrogel on SCs under oxidative stress injury and its further effect on nerve and vascular regeneration, we constructed a co-culture model in vitro. A co-culture model was constructed in vitro to deeply investigate the SC-protective effects of MSC@COP hydrogel under oxidative stress injury and further effects on neural and vascular regeneration. SCs were first incubated with H2O2 for 8 h to simulate high glucose hypoxia injury, and then the injured SCs and dorsal root ganglion (DRG) neuronal cells/HUVECs were co-cultured through 24-well transwell chambers. The SCs were inoculated with different hydrogels in the upper chamber of the transwell, and DRG cells or HUVECs were cultured in the lower chamber to investigate the activity of neuronal cells and endothelial cells (Fig. S23, Supplementary material).

DRG, as a model of primary neurons, is widely used in axon regeneration studies due to its intrinsic regenerative potential [19,54], We performed immunofluorescence staining with β-tubulin Ⅲ (DRG cell marker) and quantification of axon length after culturing DRG cells in a co-culture environment for 3 days. Compared to the Sham group (normal SCs), the mean synapse length of DRG neurons was significantly lower in the Control group, while the MSC@COP hydrogel exhibited higher axonal regeneration (Fig. 4D and S24, Supplementary material). The quantitative results indicated that the average synapse length was 33.93 μm in the Control group, which differed significantly from the values of 43.1 μm in the COP group and 66.17 μm in the MSC@COP group (Fig. 4E). Although COP did increase the synapse length of DRG neurons in the co-culture system, this increase was not statistically significant. However, it should be noted that the effect was more pronounced in the MSC@COP hydrogel, indicating that the synergistic effect of conduction and MSC resulted in enhanced nerve regeneration in vitro.

Angiogenesis is the predominant therapeutic strategy for ischemic diseases [55], and the migration and tube formation ability of vascular endothelial cells can be used to evaluate vascular regenerative capacity. As shown in Fig. S25 (Supplementary material), the migratory ability of HUVECs was assessed by cell scratch assay in the co-culture environment. After 24 h of co-incubation, the average migration rate of HUVECs co-cultured with injured SCs was significantly reduced in the Control group (20.7 %) compared with that of HUVECs co-cultured with normal SCs (58.2 %), demonstrating that the pro-angiogenic effect of SCs was inhibited by oxidative damage. In contrast, the addition of MSC@COP increased cell migration (49.7 %), demonstrating that the addition of salidroside-derived CDs reversed the SCs injury and restored its pro-angiogenic effects. In vitro tube formation experiments of HUVECs were also performed, and each group of samples was observed under a light microscope after a total of 6 h of incubation (Fig. 4F). Quantitative analysis of total tube length and number of junctions showed similar conclusions to those of mobility, i.e., oxidative damage inhibited the pro-HUVECs tube formation ability of SCs, whereas co-incubation with MSC@COP hydrogel alleviated the damage of SCs and restored their pro-vascular regenerative function (Fig. 4G and 4H).

Upon activation by injury, SCs secretes a variety of cytokines (e.g., BDNF and VEGF) coupled to neural and microvascular functions [17]. The expression of SCs gene in the co-culture system was analyzed by RT-qPCR (Fig. 4I), indicating that the expression of BDNF and VEGF was significantly reduced in oxidatively damaged SCs, but with the co-culturing of MSC@COP, the expression levels of the two cytokines were restored in SCs. Therefore, it could be assumed that the MSC@COP hydrogel ameliorated the damaged state of the SCs and re-established its functional coupling with the nerves and the microvasculature (Fig. 4J).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The SC pyroptosis inhibition of MSC@COP hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para:
We further investigated the protective effect of MSC@COP hydrogel on SCs under a high oxidative stress state by transcriptomic analysis to clarify the differences in gene expression among the Sham, H2O2, and MSC@COP groups. Specifically, principal component analysis (PCA) indicated a total variation of 93.1 % (Fig. 5
A). Volcano plots demonstrated differences in gene expression in the Sham and H2O2 groups. A total of 15,632 gene were detected between the H2O2 and MSC@COP groups, including 367 down-regulated mRNAs and 1616 up-regulated mRNAs (Fig. 5B and 5C). The Gene heatmap indicated that the expression of pyroptosis-related gene NLRP3 inflammasome was significantly increased in the H2O2 group compared to the Sham group. In contrast, MSC@COP significantly down-regulated the expression of GSDMD, Casp1, NLRP3, and IL-1β (Fig. 5D). These results indicated that MSC@COP could inhibit the pyroptosis process of SCs by suppressing the NLRP3/Casp1/GSDMD/IL-1β signaling pathway. Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) pathway enrichment analysis of the H2O2 and MSC@COP groups further showed significant enrichment of necroptosis, NLRP3 pathway, signaling regulation and cell surface receptor signaling pathways (Fig. 5E). Gene ontology (GO) analysis indicated that the pyroptosis-related genes expression of IL-1, IL-1β, and NLRP3 inflammasome was significantly down-regulated, demonstrating that inhibition of the pyroptosis pathway by hydrogel might be associated with inflammatory suppression (Fig. S26, Supplementary material). Gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA) analysis indicated that the NLR pathway was significantly enriched in the H2O2 group compared to the Sham group, but was reversed by hydrogel treatment (Fig. 5F and 5G).

Considering the association between pyroptosis and mitochondrial dysfunction, we further explored the depolarization of SCs mitochondrial membranes using JC-1 staining. The red fluorescent probe in Sham showed normal mitochondrial membrane potential, whereas the cells in the H2O2-treated Control group emitted green fluorescence, indicating that the mitochondrial potential was significantly reduced, and the mitochondrial membrane depolarization in SCs was significantly improved by co-culturing with MSC@COP (Fig. S27, Supplementary material). By immunofluorescence staining of GSDMD-N in SC, it was found that co-incubation of MSC@COP decreased the expression of GSDMD in H2O2-treated SCs (Fig. 5H and S28, Supplementary material). Finally, the expression of pyroptosis-related proteins was analyzed by western blotting. As shown in Fig. 5I and 5J, the H2O2-treated Control group showed up-regulation of NLRP3, cleaved caspase-1 and GSDMD-N, as well as expression of the inflammatory factor IL-1β, whereas MSC@COP-treated SCs showed decreased expression of these pyroptosis and inflammation-related proteins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The protective effect of MSC@COP hydrogel on diabetic CLI in vivo
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the therapeutic effect of MSC@COP hydrogel in diabetic CLI tissue, we constructed a diabetic mouse model by intraperitoneal injection of streptozotocin (50 mg kg−1, 5 days), and the diabetic CLI model was based on the diabetic model by ligating and clipping the arteries of the hind limbs (Fig. 6
A). Laser speckle perfusion imaging (LSPI) was used to evaluate the blood perfusion of the left lower limb in model mice on the 0, 7th, 14th, and 21st day after surgery (Fig. 6B). The results showed that on the 21st day, the blood flow of the conductive hydrogel COP group increased by about 13 % compared with that of the Model group. In contrast, MSC@COP hydrogel increased blood flow of more than 30 %, demonstrating that the composite hydrogel was more effective in improving perfusion to the limb (Fig. 6C). In the semi-quantitative measurements of tissue ischemia and activity injury scores, COP alleviated certain ischemia and activity limitations compared with the Model or CO group, and the synergistic effect of MSC@COP hydrogel enhanced the limb activity performance of model mice (Fig. 6D). To study the limb-preserving effect of the treatment, the outcome of limb treatment in model mice was divided into three endpoints: limb salvage, foot necrosis, and limb loss [55]. The results showed that MSC@COP hydrogel improved limb salvage while preventing necrosis and amputation in model mice (Fig. 6E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The inflammatory Microenvironmental regulation function within diabetic CLI tissues of MSC@COP hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para:
During the repair process of ischemic tissues, the effects of inflammatory environment on tissue repair function are evident throughout the cycle, especially in the early stages of injury onset [56,57]. To explore the process of inflammatory regulation of diabetic CLI by hydrogel, immunofluorescence staining of macrophages (M1 macrophage marker iNOS and M2 macrophage marker CD206) within the tissues was performed on days 3 (acute phase) and 7 (subacute phase) after the onset of injury. The results showed that during the acute phase of ischemia, a large number of M1 macrophages infiltrated in the muscle sections of the Model group and the treatment with MSC@COP hydrogel significantly reduced the number of M1 macrophages while increased the proportion of M2 macrophages. This indicated that the hydrogel could rapidly transform the pro-inflammatory microenvironment into an anti-inflammatory microenvironment and quickly enter the state of tissue repair (Fig. 7
A–C). In the subacute stages of the Model group, the infiltration of inflammatory cells in the tissue decreased, but was still dominated by M1 macrophages. In the MSC@COP group, the number of inflammatory cells was significantly reduced compared with other groups, and was dominated by M2 macrophages, which proved that the MSC@COP hydrogel had a significant effect of regulating inflammation in the early stages, and promoted the injured tissue to enter into the state of repair in advance. In the subacute stages of Model group, the degree of inflammatory cells infiltration decreased but was still dominated by M1 macrophages. In the MSC@COP group, the inflammatory cells were significantly reduced compared with other groups and was dominated by M2 macrophages, which proved that the MSC@COP hydrogel had a significant effect of regulating inflammation in the early stages and promoted the injured tissue to enter into the state of repair in advance.

We detected the immunohistochemical staining of inflammation-related cytokines (TNF-α and IL-10) within tissues. The results showed that the expression of TNF-α was significantly increased in injured tissues (Fig. 7D and 7E). The MSC@COP group showed reduced expression of TNF-α and increased the expression of the pro-inflammatory factor IL-10, which was closely related to the degree of macrophage infiltration. Further RT-qPCR was performed to detect the expression of inflammation-related factors within the homogenates of the injured muscle tissues. As shown in Fig. 7F, the gene expression of TNF-α and IL-6 was down-regulated in the MSC@COP group, whereas the gene expression of IL-10 and ARG-1 was up-regulated. These results indicated the unique anti-inflammatory ability of MSC@COP provided a favorable microenvironment for tissue repair.

ce:para.ce:italic: 3.8 MSC@COP Hydrogel Reduces Muscle Damage and Promotes Dual Neurovascular Reconstruction

ce:section.ce:para:
Hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining and Masson staining of gastrocnemius muscle tissue were performed on 21st day post-treatment to assess the ability of the treatment to protect the muscle. The results showed that the muscle tissue of the Model and CO groups showed atrophy of muscle tissues and a large number of cavities, indicating the degeneration and necrosis of this part of the muscle (Fig. 8
A, 8B, and S29, Supplementary material). In contrast, fewer tissue cavities were found in the COP and MSC@COP groups, especially in the MSC@COP group where the morphology of muscle fiber bundles was intact, suggesting that the muscle tissues in the MSC@COP group were better protected. Masson staining further demonstrated that the fibrosis area was significantly reduced in the MSC@COP group, which could significantly inhibit collagen deposition (Fig. 8C and S30, Supplementary material).

Tissue immunofluorescence staining was used to verify the effect of hydrogel on tissue SCs and neurovascular reconstruction (Fig. 8D and 8E). We first labeled SCs in tissues with SCs marker S100. The results showed that on 21st day after treatment, SC infiltration could be induced in the COP and MSC@COP groups, but the infiltration of SCs in the MSC@COP group was obvious on 7th day after treatment. Besides, the degree of infiltration was higher than that in the COP group, which proved that MSC@COP had a long-term effect on inducing SCs migration. CD31 (endothelial cell marker) and β-Tubulin Ⅲ (neuron marker) were used to evaluate the level of angiogenesis and neurogenesis in tissues, respectively. Neurovascular regeneration was assessed by tissue immunofluorescence labeling of CD31 (red) and β-Tubulin Ⅲ (green). We observed increased expression of CD31 and β-Tubulin Ⅲ in the COP and MSC@COP group, demonstrating the increased level of angiogenesis and reinnervation. Compared with the other groups at both time points, the MSC@COP group had the highest vascular and nerve density. Meanwhile, in the MSC@COP group, endothelial cells and nerves were co-localized within the tissue, and this co-localization growth pattern was similar to the structure of the neurovascular unit [58]. In the in vitro neurovascular unit model, SC acts as a key cell to stimulate the maturation of the vascular system of the neurovascular unit and regulate the growth effect of endothelial cells and neurons [59]. Therefore, based on the above results, we speculated that MSC@COP hydrogel might reshape the neurovascular microenvironment by SC-induced neurovascular unit regeneration. We further detected the protein expression of BDNF and VEGF in the tissues by western blotting. The results showed that (Fig. 8F and 8G), MSC@COP hydrogel significantly increased the expression of these two neural and vascular regulatory factors. The protein levels of pyroptosis-related proteins (NLRP3, cleaved caspase-1, GSDMD-N and IL-1β) in tissues were detected by western blotting and showed down regulation in the MSC@COP group compared with Model group (Fig. 8H and S31, Supplementary material), the result of which is consistent with the observed reduction in SCs pyroptosis in vitro, further supporting the protective effect of the conductive hydrogel on SCs.

Based on the above in vivo studies, MSC@COP hydrogel had enhanced blood perfusion and great tissue protection in diabetic CLI, which was related to inflammatory regulation, induction of SCs migration and proliferation, and remodeling of neurovascular microenvironment.

In this work, we have proposed a conductive MSC@COP hydrogel based on traditional Chinese herbal monomer-derived CDs and MS, which has low-pH responsiveness and improves the inflammatory microenvironment, as well as neurovascular reconstruction for treating diabetic CLI. Through in vivo, in vitro, and mechanism experiments, it was proven that MSC@COP hydrogel can inhibit SC pyroptosis in a diabetic CLI environment and play a dual role in neural and vascular repair. However, there are still some limitations in this study, such as the construction of the system is complex, which makes it difficult to speculate on its metabolism and toxicity, and the detailed mechanism of action in vivo remains to be further verified. Despite these limitations, the most prominent feature of this system is its neural and vascular dual reconstruction function. Considering the universal existence and repair value of nerve and vascular injury in diabetes, aging, stroke, spinal cord injury, and so on.

ce:section.ce:para:
